data.title: EQT to Buy Majority Stake in Life Science Products Distributor Europa Biosite
data.description:
EQT said Thursday its unit EQT Healthcare Growth agreed to purchase a majority stake in Swedish life science products distributor Europa Biosite from Adelis Equity.

The Swedish private equity group plans to support Europa Biosite's next growth phase through digital upgrades and expanded product access, among other things.

data.title: Catrion Catering Secures SAR36 Million Equipment Services Contract from Saudia
data.description:
Catrion Catering Holding (SASE:6004) said Thursday it signed a three-year contract to provide equipment design, procurement and supply services to Saudi Arabian Airlines Corp, or Saudia.

The contract is valued at 36 million Saudi riyals, and the Saudi airline catering and support services company expects it will positively impact its financial performance by the third quarter.

data.title: Market Chatter: TripAdvisor Investor Starboard Values Builds 9% Stake
data.description:
TripAdvisor investor Starboard Value has built a 9% stake in the company, The Wall Street Journal reported Wednesday, citing unnamed people familiar with the matter.

The activist investor run by Jeff Smith has a stake worth about $160 million based on Wednesday's prices, making it one of the top shareholders of the company.

Starboard's plans for the company could not be immediately known, the Journal reported.

The company's holdings include its travel search engine as well as a review site for experiences and a restaurant reservation service, the report said.

Tripadvisor and Starboard did not immediately reply to MT Newswires' request for comment.

data.title: Murray Income Trust Initiates Strategic Review Amid Underperformance
data.description:
Murray Income Trust's board launched a strategic review to assess the factors contributing to the company's underperformance.

The British investment trust appointed Investec Bank as the investment manager to review various strategic options available to enhance shareholder value and performance, according to a Thursday release.

Murray Income Trust said it will provide further details in due course.

data.title: BBVA Challenged With Enticing Enough Sabadell Shareholders to Support Takeover — Market Talk
data.description: Whether BBVA will win over enough Sabadell shareholders for its takeover proposal to go through remains to be seen, MKP Advisors says in a note. "The key issue is whether BBVA can enhance the offer enough to secure the required 50.01% stake whilst not upsetting its own shareholders," analysts write. The Spanish bank decided to go ahead with the bid despite the government imposing a block on a legal merger for at least three years. The difficulty lies in the composition of Sabadell's shareholder base with its large retail holding--much of it from the bank's home region of Catalonia--while BBVA isn't able to gauge the support of most other shareholders, which are institutional investors. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: HK shares end lower as tech stocks weigh on fears of e-commerce price war
data.description:
Hong Kong shares closed down on Thursday, led by tech stocks, as investors fretted that intensifying competition among e-commerce giants could squeeze profit margins.

China stocks ended higher.

** China's blue-chip CSI300 Index closed up 0.6%, while the Shanghai Composite Index was 0.2% higher. Hong Kong benchmark Hang Seng was down 0.6%.

** E-commerce giant Alibaba shares listed in Hong Kong fell nearly 3%, leading declines in Hong Kong, after the company announced a 50-billion-yuan ($6.98 billion) subsidy programme to merchants and customers on Wednesday.

** "Alibaba's plan to offer $7 bn of subsidies for food delivery and online retail implies competition is heating up again among China e-commerce companies," said UBS analysts.

** Shares of on-demand delivery giant Meituan dropped 2.5%, while JD.com fell 2.1%.

** Meanwhile, a prominent Chinese Communist Party publication called for a crackdown on competition that fuels price wars and squeezes profits in various industries, criticising large companies and local governments for unfair practices.

** China's services activity expanded at the slowest pace in nine months in June, as demand weakened and new export orders declined amid a fragile trade truce with the United States, a private sector survey showed on Thursday.

** China's semiconductor shares were little moved after U.S. chip design software developers said they have received notices lifting restrictions on exports to China.

** China is assessing the trade deal between the United States and Vietnam, and will safeguard its own rights and interests if needed, a spokesperson for China's commerce ministry said on Thursday.

** Healthcare shares led gains onshore, up 1.2%, as Beijing ramped up policy support for the country's innovative drugs.

** Biotech stocks also rose in Hong Kong, with Ascentage up 6.2%.

($1 = 7.1643 Chinese yuan renminbi)

data.title: Pri0r1ty Intelligence Group Set to Complete Halfspace Acquisition
data.description:
Pri0r1ty Intelligence Group said Thursday it will complete the acquisition of London-based sports data firm Halfspace on Friday.

The London-listed software-as-a-service company said it will issue 15,384,621 shares as initial consideration to Halfspace vendors.

The deal will help the company expand its artificial intelligence-driven services into sports and entertainment, enabling cross-selling opportunities.

data.title: Market Chatter: China's Geely Launches in UK, First Model to Be Electric EX5 SUV
data.description:
Chinese automaker Geely is launching its Geely brand in the UK, with the electric EX5 SUV set to go on sale in the fourth quarter, Reuters reported Wednesday.

The EX5 is still undergoing "intensive development work" to meet the expectations of UK car buyers and will be the first in a "diverse range of high-quality, accessible vehicles tailored for the UK," Geely said in a statement, according to Reuters.

The company joins other Chinese brands that have entered the UK market, including BYD , Chery's Omoda and Jaecoo marques, and Xpeng , the report said.

Britain is Europe's largest electric vehicle market and, unlike the European Union, has not imposed tariffs on Chinese-made EVs, Reuters said. Geely has also announced plans to launch the EX5 in several other markets, including Australia, Brazil, and Poland.

Geely's other units, Volvo Cars, Lotus, and Polestar, are already active in the UK.

data.title: Market Chatter: BYD Challenges Japanese Automakers in Southeast Asia, Says Auto Research Company
data.description:
Japanese car manufacturers are facing significant pressure in the Southeast Asian market as Chinese electric vehicle (EV) giant BYD rapidly expands its presence, Nikkei Asia reported Thursday, citing data from Tokyo-based automotive research company MarkLines.

Once dominant, Japanese brands are now seeing their long-held market share eroded, with BYD's sales in key countries like Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, and Thailand starting to rival established players such as Honda Motor and Mitsubishi Motors, said the publication.

While Toyota Motor still maintains its lead in unit sales, other Japanese automakers including Mazda Motor and Nissan Motor are feeling the intensified competition from the aggressive push of Chinese EV makers it added.

In April, BYD sold 11,404 vehicles across four Southeast Asian nations - surpassing the 10,000-unit milestone for the second consecutive month since first achieving it in March, added the publication.

Honda sold 14,433 units and Mitsubishi Motors moving 12,827 during the same period, said the report.

The Chinese EV maker is making particularly strong inroads in Indonesia and Thailand, two of the region's largest auto markets. In Indonesia, BYD's April sales of 4,307 vehicles dwarfed Honda's 3,000 units, it added.

In Thailand, BYD sold 6,338 vehicles - nearly triple Mitsubishi's 2,185 units and approaching Honda's 6,785 sales, said the news daily.

data.title: Eurozone Composite PMI Revised Higher
data.description:
The HCOB Eurozone Composite PMI rose to 50.6 in June 2025, up from both May’s reading and the preliminary estimate of 50.2.

While the latest figure indicated only modest overall growth, it marked the strongest expansion in three months, driven by faster output increases across both the manufacturing and services sectors.

Ireland led the growth rankings for the fourth consecutive month, followed by Spain, Italy, and Germany, while France continued to lag, recording a tenth straight monthly contraction in activity.

The modest rise in output was primarily supported by the completion of backlogs of work, which declined for the 27th month in a row.

Meanwhile, new business fell for the 13th consecutive month, and employment growth remained subdued.

On the price front, input cost inflation held steady at May’s six-month low.

Encouragingly, business confidence improved further in June, reaching its highest level since July 2024, continuing the recovery from April’s 18-month low.

data.title: Montana Aerospace honored twice by Airbus with prestigious SQIP awards
data.description:
Montana Aerospace

by Jürgen Beilein

Business news for the stock market

Montana Aerospace: Montana Aerospace honored twice by Airbus with prestigious SQIP awards

SQIP Award

[ Fotos /PDFs ]

Reinach (pta000/03.07.2025/10:18 UTC+2)

Universal Alloy Corporation (UAC), a division of Montana Aerospace, was recognized last week in Toulouse with two SQIP Awards – Universal Alloy Corporation Europe (UAC Europe) receiving the Accredited Award and Universal Alloy Corporation US (UAC US) receiving the Best Performer Award.

The Airbus SQIP (Supply Chain & Quality Improvement Program) Award recognizes outstanding suppliers who consistently deliver top performance in quality, on-time delivery, and continuous improvement. It is Airbus' highest recognition for supply chain excellence, highlighting partners who actively contribute to competitiveness and innovation.

"We are extremely proud to receive two SQIP Awards from Airbus this year — a clear testament to the dedication, expertise, and outstanding performance of our teams," says Kai Arndt, Co-CEO of Montana Aerospace. "It reflects our growth and our role as a reliable partner to Airbus, offering a one-stop-shop with the highest standards in process efficiency, safety, and quality. Our continuous improvement mindset is what makes us a trusted supplier for all major aerospace platforms.

Strong partnership and long-term excellence

UAC Europe with its production site in Dumbrăvița received the "Accredited Award", marking the seventh SQIP Award for UAC Europe since joining the program and highlighting the strong partnership and continuous improvement efforts shared between UAC Europe and Airbus over many years. The "Accredited Award" is the highest recognition in the Airbus SQIP program, which recognizes consistently outstanding performance for product quality, on-time delivery, continuous improvement and adaptability to product enhancements. The award is not presented every year, but only when a supplier excels.

"Being awarded the "SQIP Accredited Award" by Airbus underscores the exceptional quality and operational excellence our teams deliver every day. It is an important milestone that validates our strong focus on customer requirements and confirms our ability to meet the highest standards in the industry - all while offering a one-stop-shop approach to our customers", adds Brian Weed, CEO of UAC Europe.

Top performance through innovation

At the same time, Universal Alloy Corporation US (UAC US) with its production site in Canton received the "Best Performer Award". The selection criteria focus on suppliers who deliver top performance efficiently for at least 12 months while proactively offering innovative, customized solutions that strengthen Airbus' competitiveness in quality, delivery reliability and industrial capability.

Mike Colt, CEO of UAC US, also emphasizes the significance of this achievement: "Receiving this recognition from Airbus shows the strength of our global footprint and the unified approach. As the global leader in aluminum aerospace extrusions, Universal Alloy Corporation has made a truly impressive rise since its beginnings in 1961. This award highlights our commitment."

Commitment to growth and continuous improvement

Montana Aerospace has been a trusted supplier to Airbus for decades and recently signed a multi-year contract to further expand its support of the A330 program: UAC Europe will supply additional structural components (crossbeams) including attachments for the cabin floor.

As a vertically integrated partner, Montana Aerospace supplies components ranging from structural parts and assemblies to landing gear components and high-lift devices for all major platforms — including the Airbus A320neo and A220 models, which are among the most in-demand aircraft contributing to a projected global need for up to 44,000 new aircraft by 2044, with nearly 80 percent of that demand driven by narrowbody platforms.

In line with its growth strategy, Montana Aerospace has invested over EUR 700 million in recent years to expand its production capacities and technological competencies, significantly strengthening its global position in the aerospace industry — and today holds contracted sales worth more than EUR 7 billion.

This latest recognition from Airbus highlights Montana Aerospace's ongoing commitment to supporting the fast-growing aerospace market with advanced manufacturing solutions and underscores its position as a reliable partner focused on continuous improvement and sustainable growth.

Press photo

SQIP Award Ceremony Toulouse

© Montana Aerospace AG

About Montana Aerospace AG

Montana Aerospace AG is a leading manufacturer of system components and complex assemblies for the aerospace industry, with worldwide engineering and manufacturing operations. The Company has approximately 7,600 highly skilled employees at 22 locations on four continents – designing, developing and producing ground-breaking technologies for tomorrow's aerospace and energy industries made of aluminium, titanium, composite, copper and steel.

About Universal Alloy Corporation

Universal Alloy Corporation (UAC) is a leading global manufacturer and highly integrated producer of

structural components and assemblies for the aerospace industry. Through innovative product

development, excellent service and a worldwide manufacturing network, UAC has established itself as a market leader in the field of highly complex extruded and machined components for structural

construction, such as fuselage and wing components. With modern production facilities on three

continents – North America, Europe and Asia – UAC, a division of Montana Aerospace AG, employs around 4,300 people.

(end)

Business news for the stock market Reinach (pta/03.07.2025/10:18) - Universal Alloy Corporation (UAC), a division of Montana Aerospace, was recognized last week in Toulouse with two SQIP Awards – Universal Alloy Corporation Europe (UAC Europe) receiving the Accredited Award and Universal Alloy Corporation US (UAC US) receiving the Best Performer Award. The Airbus SQIP (Supply Chain & Quality Improvement Program) Award recognizes outstanding suppliers who consistently deliver top performance in quality, on-time delivery, and continuous improvement. It is Airbus' highest recognition for supply chain excellence, highlighting partners who actively contribute to competitiveness and innovation. "We are extremely proud to receive two SQIP Awards from Airbus this year — a clear testament to the dedication, expertise, and outstanding performance of our teams," says Kai Arndt, Co-CEO of Montana Aerospace. "It reflects our growth and our role as a reliable partner to Airbus, offering a one-stop-shop with the highest standards in process efficiency, safety, and quality. Our continuous improvement mindset is what makes us a trusted supplier for all major aerospace platforms. Strong partnership and long-term excellence UAC Europe with its production site in Dumbrăvița received the "Accredited Award", marking the seventh SQIP Award for UAC Europe since joining the program and highlighting the strong partnership and continuous improvement efforts shared between UAC Europe and Airbus over many years. The "Accredited Award" is the highest recognition in the Airbus SQIP program, which recognizes consistently outstanding performance for product quality, on-time delivery, continuous improvement and adaptability to product enhancements. The award is not presented every year, but only when a supplier excels. "Being awarded the "SQIP Accredited Award" by Airbus underscores the exceptional quality and operational excellence our teams deliver every day. It is an important milestone that validates our strong focus on customer requirements and confirms our ability to meet the highest standards in the industry - all while offering a one-stop-shop approach to our customers", adds Brian Weed, CEO of UAC Europe. Top performance through innovation At the same time, Universal Alloy Corporation US (UAC US) with its production site in Canton received the "Best Performer Award". The selection criteria focus on suppliers who deliver top performance efficiently for at least 12 months while proactively offering innovative, customized solutions that strengthen Airbus' competitiveness in quality, delivery reliability and industrial capability. Mike Colt, CEO of UAC US, also emphasizes the significance of this achievement: "Receiving this recognition from Airbus shows the strength of our global footprint and the unified approach. As the global leader in aluminum aerospace extrusions, Universal Alloy Corporation has made a truly impressive rise since its beginnings in 1961. This award highlights our commitment." Commitment to growth and continuous improvement Montana Aerospace has been a trusted supplier to Airbus for decades and recently signed a multi-year contract to further expand its support of the A330 program: UAC Europe will supply additional structural components (crossbeams) including attachments for the cabin floor. As a vertically integrated partner, Montana Aerospace supplies components ranging from structural parts and assemblies to landing gear components and high-lift devices for all major platforms — including the Airbus A320neo and A220 models, which are among the most in-demand aircraft contributing to a projected global need for up to 44,000 new aircraft by 2044, with nearly 80 percent of that demand driven by narrowbody platforms. In line with its growth strategy, Montana Aerospace has invested over EUR 700 million in recent years to expand its production capacities and technological competencies, significantly strengthening its global position in the aerospace industry — and today holds contracted sales worth more than EUR 7 billion. This latest recognition from Airbus highlights Montana Aerospace's ongoing commitment to supporting the fast-growing aerospace market with advanced manufacturing solutions and underscores its position as a reliable partner focused on continuous improvement and sustainable growth. The Company has approximately 7,600 highly skilled employees at 22 locations on four continents – designing, developing and producing ground-breaking technologies for tomorrow's aerospace and energy industries made of aluminium, titanium, composite, copper and steel. Through innovative product development, excellent service and a worldwide manufacturing network, UAC has established itself as a market leader in the field of highly complex extruded and machined components for structural construction, such as fuselage and wing components. With modern production facilities on three continents – North America, Europe and Asia – UAC, a division of Montana Aerospace AG, employs around 4,300 people. data.title: BayWa says equity capital falls to less than half its registered share capital
data.description:
German agricultural supplies trader BayWa said on Thursday that its equity capital had turned negative as of the end of 2024, falling to less than half of its registered share capital.

The drop was expected due to the necessary write-downs brought on as part of BayWa's restructuring plan, said the company, which will report annual results on July 10 after being delayed due to its financial concerns.

The Munich-based firm has been grappling with rising borrowing costs, forcing it to embark on a restructuring plan, including job cuts.

Due to the write-downs, BayWa's management board expects a net loss of 1.6 billion euros ($1.89 billion) for the financial year 2024, it said in a statement.

This will have no impact on the implementation of the restructuring plan, which was confirmed by a court in June, and on the restructuring of its financing until 2028, it said.

Equity capital represents the total value of a company attributable to equity investors and is calculated by subtracting total liabilities from total assets.

According to German law, a company's management board must convene a general meeting to inform its investors when the it registers a loss amounting to half of the capital stock.

BayWa said it would discuss the situation during its annual general meeting on August 26.

($1 = 0.8470 euros)

The 60-megawatt Duvhällen wind project, based in Eskilstuna, Sweden, is expected to be operational in 2028, the renewable energy development company said Thursday.

data.title: Dollar steady as traders await US payroll data
data.description:
Key points:
  • Dollar near 3-1/2-year low ahead of payrolls data
  • Employment report could help shape US rate path
  • Pound steadies after drop on fiscal worries
  • U.S. House of Republicans advance tax-cut bill

The dollar remained close to this week's 3-1/2 year lows on Thursday ahead of a key jobs report, and as a U.S.-Vietnam trade accord fanned expectations for other potential deals ahead of July 9 when U.S. tariffs take effect.

Sterling inched up after a nearly 1% drop on Wednesday, when UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer's office backed finance minister Rachel Reeves after rumours she would be dismissed over investor worries about Britain's finances.

British government bonds stabilized after a selloff on Wednesday fuelled by a tearful appearance by Reeves in parliament a day after the government backed down on welfare reforms.

The pound edged higher by 0.2% and last fetched $1.3665, while the euro was muted at $1.180, still near the September 2021 top it hit earlier this week. The yen was a tad weaker at 143.80 per dollar.

Currency strategist Carol Kong at Commonwealth Bank of Australia said market participants are worried Reeves could be replaced with someone less committed to the government's self-imposed fiscal rules and more willing to borrow.

"The pound can remain under downward pressure unless the U.K. government takes measures to restore market confidence in U.K. finances."

The U.S. dollar index , which measures the greenback against six other currencies, was flat at 96.748, remaining close to the 3-1/2-year lows it has been rooted to this week. The index is on course for a 0.5% drop for the week.

All eyes are on the U.S. Labor Department's comprehensive employment report for June, due for release on Thursday ahead of the July 4 holiday, which is expected to show that unemployment edged up to a more than a 3-1/2-year high of 4.3%, according to economists polled by Reuters.

Wednesday's private survey painted a grim picture of the labor market, pushing traders to shift expectations of when the Federal Reserve will lower interest rates. Traders are pricing in a 25% chance of a cut in July versus 19% a day earlier, data compiled by LSEG showed.

Today's data "will bring growth concerns firmly back into the spotlight and pressure on the Fed to accelerate its timetable is only going to increase," said Max McKechnie, global market strategist at J.P. Morgan Asset Management.

"With the inflation side of its mandate still further away from target than the employment side, the Fed should stick to its guns. The bar for easing should remain higher than a single weaker labour market print."

TRADE DEALS

Ahead of the July 9 tariff deadline, U.S. President Donald Trump said the United States had struck a deal with Vietnam and that he could push other countries to reach similar agreements.

Although details were scant, Trump said Vietnamese goods would face a 20% tariff and trans-shipments from third countries through Vietnam will face a 40% levy.

The Vietnamese dong slid to a record low, with UBS analysts suggesting the passing of tariff costs to exporters will likely be mitigated by the central bank through the allowance of a steady depreciation of the dong.

Progress on other deals has been slow. Japan has invoked national interests as talks with the U.S. struggled, while South Korea's President Lee Jae Myung on Thursday said negotiations were looking difficult and that he could not say whether talks would conclude by next Tuesday.

Meanwhile, Republicans in the U.S. House of Representatives cleared a procedural hurdle to advance Trump's massive tax-cut and spending bill, paving the way for a debate and final vote on the highly contested legislation.

The bill is set to add $3.3 trillion to an already swelling national debt, stoking fiscal worries. Bond investors around the world are growing increasingly nervous about government deficits in countries including Japan and the United States.

data.title: Hang Seng Pares Losses at Close
data.description:
The Hang Seng dipped 151 points or 0.6% to finish at 24,070 on Thursday, reversing previous strength as falls hit most sectors, particularly consumer, financial, and tech.

Caution prevailed ahead of key U.S. payroll data after the first drop in private sector jobs in over two years.

In China, a private survey showed services activity fell to a nine-month low in June, weighed by the steepest decline in foreign sales in over two years amid mounting trade headwinds.

Fierce competition among mainland e-commerce giants also raised concerns over profit margins.

The index trimmed early losses on an uptick in U.S. futures after Trump’s trade deal with Vietnam.

Meantime, U.S. chip design firms reported easing export curbs to China, and the U.S. House narrowly passed Trump’s tax and spending bill.

Alibaba slid near 3% after launching a CNY 50 billion subsidy plan, while Meituan and JD.com lost 2.4% and 2.3%, respectively.

Pharma stocks advanced on fresh policy support for innovative drugs.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0810 GMT - Southeast Asia is becoming a global hub for artificial intelligence, says Google's Sapna Chadha. Amid the "polarized world" of geopolitics, the region is instead developing frameworks around AI ethics and coming together with discussions to drive innovation faster than other parts of the world, the Google vice president says during a panel at the 9th Asean Conference organized by SBF. Companies don't pick sides in the region, making it a unique space for opportunity, Chadha says. According to Google's research, about 70% of businesses in the region see return on investment in AI in less than six months, and the pace of innovation is a reason why Google is heavily investing in infrastructure in the region, Chadha adds. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0141 GMT - Malaysia's tech sector outlook may remain cautious as earnings recovery faces headwinds, including weak non-AI demand, CIMB Securities analyst Mohd Shanaz Noor Azam says in a note. While AI-related chip demand and smartphone sales remain strong, broader sector growth is uneven, he says. Upside catalysts include resilient hyperscaler spending, China's chip localization push, and rising defense tech demand. However, currency volatility, rising operating costs, and potential demand volatility amid the end of the U.S. reciprocal tariff grace period could be near-term headwinds, he says. CIMB maintains a neutral rating on Malaysia's tech sector, and selects Malaysian Pacific Industries and Inari Amertron as preferred picks due to their strong fundamentals. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1704 GMT - Almost half--22--of the 50 largest U.S. metros have more active listings on the market than they did pre-pandemic, according Realtor.com's Monthly Housing Report. Denver saw the largest recovery, with a 100% increase in available homes compared to 2017-2019 averages, followed by Austin, with a 69% increase, and Seattle, with a 60.9% rise. Realtor.com says that in some areas, affordability concerns have also slowed buyer demand, giving the market "room to breathe and contributing to gains in homes for sale." Markets that built the most new housing during and after the pandemic are now the most likely to show inventory gains. These cities now face longer selling times, both of which contribute to rising inventory. Buyers are moving more cautiously in 2025, and in many Western and Southern metros, homes are sitting longer. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1451 GMT - Infineon Technologies is expected to raise guidance for fiscal 2025 when it reports results for its fiscal third quarter on Aug. 5, UBS analysts write in a research note. The German chip maker in May cut its projections for the year ending Sept. 30, citing tariff uncertainty and currency headwinds. It expects revenue in the fiscal year to decline slightly compared with the 14.96 billion euros it reported for fiscal 2024. However, the analysts are expecting Infineon to upgrade its revenue forecast to slight growth on year. They say demand for chips used in vehicles and industrial equipment recovered more than expected. Infineon shares trade 1.6% higher at 36.07 euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

1417 GMT - STMicroelectronics is expected to report a strong set of second-quarter results and provide upbeat guidance for the third quarter, UBS analysts write in a note to clients. The European chip maker could guide for third-quarter revenue growth of 12% compared with the second quarter, the analysts say. Improving demand for chips in vehicles and industrial equipment as well as smartphones could drive that growth, they say. Chief Executive Jean-Marc Chery said at an event in June that a recovery in demand would benefit the third quarter unless trade tensions get in the way. STMicroelectronics reports second-quarter results on July 24. STMicroelectronics shares trade 4.5% higher at 26.64 euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

1339 GMT - Wedbush expects more M&A in the building materials distribution sector given the underlying market dynamics. Home Depot this week announced it will buy building-products distributor GMS for about $4.3 billion, winning a bidding war with roofing-products distributor QXO. Analyst Jay McCanless notes that the government's monthly new residential sales report shows new residential sales are equivalent to home builder orders. He believes outsized growth among the larger builders is a key reason QXO is focused on buying and improving larger distribution businesses. "Assuming the larger builders continue taking share, we believe those companies will need distribution partners that can increase their scope and scale to service those builders," says McCanless. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1206 GMT - D.A. Davidson believes TikTok parent ByteDance is the likely customer that signed a big cloud agreement with Oracle. Earlier this week Oracle disclosed that a new deal is expected to contribute more than $30 billion in annual revenue. Analysts Gil Luria and Alexander Platt in a research note say that a deal of this size can only be a few customers at best, leaving ByteDance or OpenAI and as the likely candidates. The analysts think recent news that a TikTok divestiture from ByteDance is back on the table supports the idea of an expanded relationship with Oracle, which has been linked as a potential buyer. If a deal happens, the analysts think it's likely that TikTok data centers would be hosted by Oracle, and ByteDance would utilize Oracle for non-US social media and research lab compute capacity. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1051 GMT - ASML Holding's short-term outlook remain uncertain, UBS analysts write in a note. Investor sentiment has improved recently, supported by growing optimism around AI and stronger data from smartphone and PC sales. But orders are expected to remain weak in the second quarter. The sustainability of demand from China also remains a concern, they say. The Swiss bank forecasts China revenue to decline 35% on year this quarter. Additionally, the Dutch semiconductor-equipment maker is unlikely to issue guidance for 2026 at this stage, they add. Shares are down 0.8% at 662.90 euros. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

1009 GMT - Sage Group's third-quarter organic growth is expected to remain broadly stable, J.P. Morgan Cazenove analysts write in a note. The U.S. investment bank forecasts organic total revenue growth of around 9% on year, in line with the first half and consensus. However, they note that consensus estimates may need minor adjustments. They expect slightly lower recurring revenue than previously forecast. At the same time, other revenue segments appear more resilient, they add. Shares are down 3.6% at 1,186 pence.(najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0930 GMT - Vodafone Group's network quality continues to lag behind peers, Morgan Stanley analysts write in a note. While the merger with rival Three in the U.K. could help narrow the gap in that market, network quality should continue to be a weakness in other regions, they say. Additionally, the German market remains a significant challenge due to recent substantial market share losses, they add. "There is still considerable work left to do," they say. Shares are down 0.2% at 79.56 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0848 GMT - Xiaomi's EV production capacity is the key bottleneck amid strong orders for its latest SUV, HSBC Global Research analysts write in a note. It would take more than three months for Xiaomi to ramp up its Yu7 production, leveraging both its phase 1 and phase 2 manufacturing plants, they say. The brokerage forecasts around 50,000 YU7 deliveries by September and a normalized run-rate of more than 20,000 vehicles a month in 4Q, they add. The YU7's prices were below market expectations, ranging from CNY253,500 to CNY329,900. The stronger-than-expected demand for the new model is likely due to competitive pricing, specs and a variety of customization solutions, they say. HSBC Global Research maintains its buy rating for the stock and raises the target price to HK$80.40 from HK$76.60. Shares last closed at HK$60.15. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0745 GMT - China's efforts to become more innovative in healthcare are bearing fruit, creating opportunities for global investors, ClearBridge analysts write in a note. China is investing heavily in research and development to create its own therapeutics through a homegrown biotechnology industry, they say. China isn't simply trying to improve domestic medical care, it is also seeking to claim a profitable share of the global biopharmaceutical market, they add. China arguably has greater life sciences infrastructure and institutional support compared with developed markets that currently dominate clinical development and sales of prescription drugs, they say. China also has a large talent pool in the sector and the paths to profitability in the Chinese biotech industry are evolving rapidly, they add. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

1655 GMT - Higher costs across Centene's business are pressuring the company, Deutsche analyst George Hill says. Along with withdrawing its guidance on worse-than-expected marketplace data Tuesday, the health-insurance provider's Medicaid business is facing elevated medical costs, Hill says. It now expects the second-quarter Medicaid health benefit ratio — which is the percentage of premiums spent on medical care — to be higher than the first quarter's 94% rate. Hill estimates Medicare Advantage and Prescription Drug Plan will both lose money in 2025, even though they are tracking ahead of expectations in 2Q. Centene shares fall 40%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1507 GMT - The luxury sector should see a divergence in company performance in the first half of the year, but overall the industry will see a slowdown in growth, HSBC Global Research analysts write in a note to clients. President Trump's announcement of tariffs hurt luxury demand, before what is expected to have been a better performance in May and June, the analysts say. Furthermore, growth in Japan--which had been boosted a year ago by the weak Japanese yen--will have likely reversed visibly for most luxury groups, they add. On a positive note, spending by Chinese consumers, who once drove the sector's growth but are now saving rather than spending money on luxuries, could start to rebound later in the year, the analysts say. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

1226 GMT - Centene tumbles after withdrawing guidance for 2025, and Mizuho analysts believe it's possible its previous FY adjusted EPS outlook could eventually be cut in half. The managed-care specialist, which focuses largely on Medicaid and the Affordable Care Act insurance marketplaces, withdrew guidance after receiving data showing that enrollees in the marketplaces overall appeared fewer, and less healthy, than expected. The analysts believe a key issue could be a structural shift in the cost profile of the individual health exchange population due to Medicaid redeterminations. "Although we are not changing our estimate, we do not believe it is out of the realm of possibility that Centene's current 2025E adjusted EPS guide of at least $7.25 is cut in half," they add. Centene dives 30% to $39.28 premarket. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0933 GMT - Innovent Biologics's weight management drug Mazdutide receiving approval from China's national medical products administration is the company's second most important revenue-contributing aspect over the next five years, equity analyst Yurou Zheng from Morningstar writes in a note. With approval pending, the brokerage expects revenue contribution to exceed CNY600 million this year, she adds. Mazdutide is likely to reach peak sales of CNY3.5 billion in 2029, contributing around 20% of the company's total revenue, Zheng says. Morningstar maintains its fair value estimate at HK$58.10. Shares last closed at HK$79.30. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0806 GMT - Asean remains an attractive investment destination despite disruptions from tariffs and geopolitical tensions, says UOB deputy chairman and chief executive Wee Ee Cheong. Trade remains a critical economic driver for the region, Wee said at the 9th ASEAN Conference. "We see opportunities for the rapidly expanding digital economy and new investments in climate and sustainability." AI and ESG are now central to government and business strategies - a shift that is creating strong opportunities for public-private partnerships to build future-ready infrastructure, Wee said. Initiatives such as the Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone show how collaboration can unlock new opportunities, Wee added.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0754 GMT - Vietnamese equities may become more positive for investors as its latest trade deal with the U.S. removes a key overhang for the market, Julius Baer equity analyst Jen-Ai Chua writes in a note. The U.S. plans to charge a 20% tariff on any and all goods sent to the U.S. from Vietnam, and a 40% tariff on transshipments through the country. The accelerated opening of the Vietnamese market to the U.S. should be positive for its economic growth and international capital flows, Chua adds. While the Vietnamese stock exchange is massively outperforming its Southeast Asian counterparts, positive sentiment and price momentum could carry the market further, the analyst says. ETFs would be the best way to invest in Vietnamese equities given existing constraints on direct equity purchases by foreign investors, Chua says. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0703 GMT - Sabadell selling its U.K. unit TSB to Santander doesn't change the strategic rationale of BBVA's takeover bid as the asset wasn't seen as strategic in the first place, Jefferies says in a research note. "It remains to be seen how competitive BBVA's current bid is in light of the [circa 50%] premium at which SAB agreed to sell TSB," analysts write. Given the nature of the all-stock offer, the current terms of the deal value Sabadell at an around 10% discount to market value, they say. For buyer Santander, which already operates a bank in the country, the operational risk of the deal is limited given the similar and "vanilla" business models of the two entities, they say. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0104 GMT - While Macquarie's earnings outlook is improving, short-term consensus forecasts remain too high, according to Morgan Stanley analysts. They lift their fiscal 2026 net profit forecast by 2.5% and now anticipate 8% on-year growth, but tell clients in a note that they see this skewed toward the second half. They forecast 6% on-year profit growth for the Australian financial giant's fiscal first half, warning that the average analyst forecast of 20% growth looks much too high. More broadly, they think that the recent recovery in capital markets is supportive and expect global M&A activity to recover 10% in 2025. MS raises its target price 8.5% to A$216.00 and maintains an equal-weight rating. Shares are down 1.2% at A$226.26. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0049 GMT - Australian bank deposits are likely to keep growing over the next three years thanks to continued high levels of government spending, Macquarie analysts reckon. They expect annual credit growth to moderate toward 5% from about 7%, but see government spending supporting annual deposit growth at about 6%-7% for the next three years. They tell clients in a note that in this scenario, deposit growth and funding costs could remain supportive for major banks. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

0041 GMT - Mortgage insurer Helia shakes its bear at Macquarie following the share-price dive that met the likely loss of a second major contract in recent months. The investment bank's analysts think the Australian company will retain just a third of its current gross written premium if it does end up losing its contracts with ING and Commonwealth Bank. However, they point out in a note to clients that Helia's back book will keep generating capital and that there should still be opportunities for the company to return capital until fiscal 2028. Macquarie raises its target price 3.1% to A$3.35 and lifts its recommendation to neutral from underperform. Shares are up 3.7% at A$4.47. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2225 GMT - Australian mortgage insurer Helia says the loss of ING as a customer increases the scope for more capital management. Jefferies assesses what that might mean. Helia held regulatory capital of roughly A$1.76 billion at its FY 2024 result. That was well above a requirement of A$837 million. Jefferies estimates around 48% of Helia's capital is associated with providing insurance services to Commonwealth Bank of Australia and another 18% with ING. Helia's contract with CBA expires at the end of this year. "Whilst this points to a large portion of capital being returned to shareholders over time, buybacks will be limited by liquidity," analyst Simon Fitzgerald says. "We believe management will need to consider special dividends in order to return capital more efficiently." (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1704 GMT - Almost half--22--of the 50 largest U.S. metros have more active listings on the market than they did pre-pandemic, according Realtor.com's Monthly Housing Report. Denver saw the largest recovery, with a 100% increase in available homes compared to 2017-2019 averages, followed by Austin, with a 69% increase, and Seattle, with a 60.9% rise. Realtor.com says that in some areas, affordability concerns have also slowed buyer demand, giving the market "room to breathe and contributing to gains in homes for sale." Markets that built the most new housing during and after the pandemic are now the most likely to show inventory gains. These cities now face longer selling times, both of which contribute to rising inventory. Buyers are moving more cautiously in 2025, and in many Western and Southern metros, homes are sitting longer. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1611 GMT - Analysts disagree on just how much it will help the Treasury market if federal regulators tweak the level of capital bankers must reserve against the government securities they own. Money-market portfolio manager Michael Bird of Allspring thinks such reform could give the market a boost. "I think the ability for banks to carve out their U.S. Treasury holdings [from regulatory leverage ratios] is important," Bird tells WSJ. "It's crucial that gets passed." It's among one of the changes regulators at the Fed and other federal bank-oversight offices are weighing this summer as they aim to bolster liquidity in the roughly $29 trillion market. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1434 GMT - The slump in Canada's housing market is likely to extend through the end of 2025 unless an immediate deal is reach to remove most U.S.-Canada tariffs, Oxford Economics' Tony Stillo reckons. Canada's resale market remains in stasis, Stillo says, with buyers and sellers paralyzed by uncertainty from the trade war. "Poor affordability, weak confidence, and job losses from the recession now likely underway, on top of headwinds from a shrinking population, will continue to weigh on demand. The economist adds this will also push up the supply of homes for sale, causing average prices to fall a cumulative 8%-10% by the end of 2025. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1226 GMT - Centene tumbles after withdrawing guidance for 2025, and Mizuho analysts believe it's possible its previous FY adjusted EPS outlook could eventually be cut in half. The managed-care specialist, which focuses largely on Medicaid and the Affordable Care Act insurance marketplaces, withdrew guidance after receiving data showing that enrollees in the marketplaces overall appeared fewer, and less healthy, than expected. The analysts believe a key issue could be a structural shift in the cost profile of the individual health exchange population due to Medicaid redeterminations. "Although we are not changing our estimate, we do not believe it is out of the realm of possibility that Centene's current 2025E adjusted EPS guide of at least $7.25 is cut in half," they add. Centene dives 30% to $39.28 premarket. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1158 GMT - Santander's acquisition of Sabadell's TSB unit in the U.K. won't materially improve its position in that market but should enable it to solve its profitability issue through strong efficiency gains, AlphaValue says in a research note. Sabadell sold its U.K. arm for 2.65 billion pounds in cash to Santander, which expects the deal to be 4% earnings per share accretive in 2028. The deal reinforces Santander's position in the U.K., which doesn't surprise since it had reportedly rejected an approach for NatWest for its own operations, analyst David Grinsztajn writes. "Unfortunately, the expected EPS accretion is not material enough for us to change our neutral stance," he notes. Santander shares rise 3% while Sabadell's are up 5%. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0506 GMT - ACEN Corp. faces earnings hit from near-term headwinds, Maybank Securities' Germaine Guinto says in a research report. The energy company's exposure to the Philippines' electricity spot market has risen to 53% as of 1Q from 23% at end-2024, the analyst notes. This greater unhedged exposure makes the Philippine company more vulnerable to market-price volatility. At the same time, domestic generation is likely to fall owing to lower solar irradiance and temporary outages. The brokerage cuts its 2025 and 2026 net-income estimates for ACEN Corp. by 22% and 32%, respectively. It lowers the stock's target price to PHP3.67 from PHP4.98, with an unchanged buy rating. Shares are 2.3% higher at PHP2.65. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0230 GMT - Malaysia's utilities firms are set to post stronger earnings momentum in 2H, driven by robust electricity demand from data-center expansion and the rollout of a new base electricity tariff, Kenanga IB analyst Teh Kian Yeong says. He thinks the sector is well-positioned for growth, noting upcoming bids for new power capacity projects and potential gas plant conversions. A third regasification terminal is also in the works to meet rising gas demand, he adds. Despite persistent market uncertainties, Teh thinks the sector remains a solid investment choice due to stable and resilient earnings. Kenanga stays overweight on Malaysia utilities, naming Tenaga Nasional as its top pick for its long-term growth prospects. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0214 GMT - Tenaga Nasional's legal setback in its 1.25 billion ringgit tax dispute could set a precedent for its other pending cases, totaling 5.05 billion ringgit, CIMB Securities analyst Foong Choong Chen says in a note. In the worst-case scenario, CIMB may lower the target price by 5.5%, but this impact may narrow if Tenaga succeeds in claiming investment allowances. Currently, Tenaga is trading at a fair valuation based on expected 2025 earnings and is projected to deliver steady dividend yields of 3.1% to 3.8% over 2025 to 2027, he adds. CIMB Securities maintains a buy rating on Tenaga and keeps its target price at MYR15.75, pending further clarity on the allowance claim and broader financial implications. Shares are 4.4% lower at MYR13.96. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0116 GMT - The overhang from Tenaga Nasional's tax dispute over its 2018 assessment of MYR1.25 billion is expected to prolong following the Federal Court's ruling in favor of the Inland Revenue Board, Maybank IB analyst Tan Chi Wei says in a note. The case may affect other assessment years under review, but the potential provisioning is seen as a one-off, while the overall dispute amount appears manageable given Tenaga's strong cash reserves. Tan estimates that Tenaga has MYR5.05 billion worth of outstanding tax disputes, representing around 6% of its market capitalization, or MYR0.87 a share. Maybank maintains a buy rating on Tenaga and keeps the target price at MYR15.50. Shares are 3.0% lower at MYR14.16. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0039 GMT - Some risk premium is likely to linger in the oil price into 3Q before the market reverts back to fundamentals, UBS says. Global oil supply-demand balances point to an oversupplied market. UBS sees a surplus of oil in the market growing from 300,000 b/d in 3Q this year to 1.8 million b/d in 1Q of 2026. "The incremental driver of oversupply comes from rising OPEC+ output as an unwinding of voluntary quota barrels continues," UBS says. Brent crude--the global oil-price benchmark--is likely to settle into the low to mid US$60s per barrel in the near term, UBS says. Lower prices are likely to drive a supply response, particularly from U.S. producers. Brent ended Wednesday at US$69.11/bbl.(david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

0022 GMT - Amplitude Energy could beat its annual target for oil and natural gas production, Bell Potter says. It points to data from the Australian Energy Market Operator that suggests Amplitude's two key natural-gas assets produced a net 26 petajoules in FY 2025. When combined with oil and condensate this "should see the company reach the top end or even exceed guidance of 25.2-26.6 petajoules equivalent," analyst Stuart Howe says. Bell Potter retains a buy call on the stock while lifting its price target by 3.8% to A$0.27/share. Amplitude is unchanged at A$0.21 today. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

0008 GMT - Oil edges lower in the early Asian session amid worries over further scaling back of output cuts by OPEC+. The group seems poised to unwind production quotas by another roughly 400,000 barrels a day in August, when it meets on July 6, Citi Research's Francesco Martoccia says in a research report. Recent sharp swings in oil prices suggest financial positioning should have returned to neutral levels. This may provide scope for a limited and brief sell-off on OPEC+ headlines of another 411,000 barrels-per-day quota unwind, the strategist adds. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are down 0.2% at $67.31/bbl; front-month Brent crude oil futures are 0.2% lower at $68.96/bbl. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2013 GMT - Oil and gas companies polled by the Dallas Fed expect West Texas Intermediate crude oil to be at $68 a barrel at the end of the year, practically unchanged from average expectations in the first quarter. Year-end price estimates in the 2Q survey, conducted June 18-26, ranged from $50 to $85 a barrel, compared with $50-$100 in 1Q. On average, respondents see WTI at $72 two years from now and $77 five years from now. Henry Hub natural gas prices are seen at $3.66/mmBtu at end-2025, $4.12 in two years and $4.50 in five years. Nymex WTI for August delivery settled at $67.45 a barrel and Nymex natural gas at $3.488/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1909 GMT - Oil futures post their sharpest gains in two weeks after Iran said it's suspending cooperation with the U.N. atomic agency, returning some of the risk premium that faded following last week's cease-fire. The market seemingly ignored a spate of bearish factors, including an unexpected 3.8 million barrel build in U.S. crude stocks, a private-sector report showing the U.S. lost jobs in June, and the approach of the OPEC+ meeting that's expected to confirm another large output increase in August. There was also 1 million barrel-a-day drop in U.S. gasoline demand. "Any time gasoline demand is down by over a million is a negative event for the oil industry," Mizuho's Robert Yawger says in a note. "When it happens in the midst of summer driving season it is a negative event with an exclamation point!" WTI settles up 3.1% at $67.45 a barrel and Brent rises 3% to $69.11. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1808 GMT - As the U.S. gears up to mark the 249th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence, the EIA takes a look at how energy use has changed over two-and-a-half centuries. Back in 1776, wood--a renewable resource--was used for heating, cooking and lighting, the agency says. Wood was overtaken by coal around 1885, and that was later surpassed by petroleum, which has been the main energy source since 1950. Natural gas overtook coal in 1958 and is the second-largest energy source. More recently, renewables including wind and solar power, as well as biofuels, have tied with nuclear power at 9% and overtaken coal, which accounts for 8% of energy use. Wood energy is still consumed, the EIA adds, "mainly by industrial lumber and paper plants that burn excess wood waste to generate electricity."(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1619 GMT - A fourth straight weekly draw from crude oil stocks at Cushing, Okla., to their lowest level since January helps bolster oil prices despite a surprise 3.8 million-barrel overall U.S. stock build. Cushing is the delivery point for futures contracts traded on Nymex, and the largest storage site in the U.S. The pickup in prices following a mostly bearish EIA inventory report was "a reaction to Cushing, not looking at the bigger number for broader crude," says Mizuho's Robert Yawger. The stock decline at Cushing is partly due to lower imports from Canada following the tariff disputes in April, he says. "Some of those barrels that make their way to Cushing are not going there." WTI is up 1.5% at $66.42 a barrel. Brent is 1.6% higher at $68.16. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1517 GMT - Oil futures are off earlier highs as the market awaits weekend confirmation of another large OPEC+ output increase for August and digests the ADP report showing 33,000 private-sector job losses in June. "We believe the market's focus remains on the upcoming OPEC+ meeting," Spartan Capital's Peter Cardillo says in a note. "However, if the official labor market report confirms that the market has lost jobs for the first time since 2023, oil prices are likely to tumble to $60 as demand concerns intensify." WTI is up 0.6% at $65.85 a barrel, and Brent is up 0.8% at $67.63. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0731 GMT - Samsung Heavy Industries could achieve its 2025 goal for new contract wins despite a sluggish start, DB Financial Investment analyst Seo Jae-ho writes in a note. Fresh orders for liquefied natural gas carriers should be placed starting from 2H after the U.S. government recently approved a series of LNG projects, Seo says. Some of these projects are due to start as early as in 2027. Japan's recent move to increase LNG imports should also fuel demand for LNG carriers, he adds. The South Korean shipbuilder, which bagged $2.6 billion of new orders in 1H, aims to secure $9.8 billion of new contract wins for this year, he notes.(kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

2111 GMT - Lucid Group reports delivering 3,309 vehicles in 2Q and producing 3,863 vehicles. Both figures miss the estimates of Cantor analysts Andres Sheppard and Anand Balaji, who had been looking for 3,791 deliveries and 4,000 produced vehicles. Analysts polled by FactSet had been expecting Lucid to produce 5,300 vehicles during the quarter. The 3,863 tally is still an improvement from 2,110 in the same quarter a year earlier, the Cantor analysts say. They add that Lucid's guidance for producing 20,000 vehicles this year remains unchanged and in line with their own projections. Shares remain flat at $2.05 after hours. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1100 GMT - It is currently unclear how carbon-dioxide emissions can be reduced by 90% across Europe by 2040, Hildegard Mueller, head of the German Association of the Automotive Industry, says in a statement. "Brussels must do more than simply set or propose ever more ambitious targets; the European Union must ensure that these can actually be achieved," Mueller says. "Regulation alone is not policy." Nevertheless, the association stands by the Paris climate protection goals, Mueller says. The EU also targets a 55% reduction by 2030. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

1046 GMT - Ryanair will likely report record first-quarter results on July 21, including more than double last year's net profit of 360 million euros, Davy analysts Stephen Furlong and Ava Costello say. A late Easter helped, but other factors include strong demand and a tight supply market, they say. Investors will closely watch whether this momentum continues over the summer. Boeing's supply chain issues have improved, with Ryanair likely to receive the bulk of the remaining 29 B737-8200 aircraft before the end of this calendar year, the analysts add. Shares are up 0.8% at 24.01 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

1018 GMT - Renault is likely to outline a better earnings-per-share forecast for fiscal 2025 than peers, Citi says. The French carmaker's new product offensive continues to support earnings in terms of volume and mix. This has offset negative country mix, foreign exchange rates, sales and pricing, Citi says. "The recent share price decline on the loss of the CEO and a deemed Nissan share overhang to us feels overdone and could represent a good second half of 2025 trading entry point," Citi analysts say. The company continues to execute, while the balance sheet is improving and China and tariff risks remain low, they add. Citi maintains its buy recommendation and a 52 euro target price. Shares trade 2.3% higher at 41.11 euros.(nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0958 GMT - Li Auto's weaker-than-expected sales order may weigh on its earnings and revenue this year, Deutsche Bank Bin Wang writes in a note. The company's June deliveries fell 24% on year to 36,279 units, lower than its original guidance of between 482,000 to 532,000 units, Wang says. The company attributed the sales volume decline to its sales system upgrade, Wang adds. The organization restructuring negatively affected Li Auto's near-term sales performance, although this might positively benefit its sales in the medium and long term, DB says. The brokerage cut its 2025 revenue and net profit estimates by 7.1% and 18.9%, respectively. DB maintains a buy rating for the stock but lowers the target price to HK$145.00 from HK$148.00. Shares last closed at HK$103.60.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0952 GMT - Mid-cap stocks in Singapore with an average daily trading volume of at least S$2 million could be key beneficiaries of coming measures to boost the local stock market, UOB Kay Hian analysts say in a note. Institutional fund managers tapping into the Monetary Authority of Singapore's S$5 billion Equity Market Development Program are likely to prefer stocks with high liquidity and strong fundamentals, such as EPS growth and yields of more than 3% over the past five years. Funds from the program could be deployed as soon as 4Q, the analysts add. The brokerage's top picks are wealth management platform iFast, dormitory operator Centurion and Singapore Post. However, they caution that a continuing asset sale could reduce the conglomerate to only a postal services company. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0904 GMT - Schaeffler has the potential to be the sector's most interesting story, but this depends on the path of profitability in e-mobility, Jefferies analysts say in a research note. Elsewhere in the business, the German auto supplier has cost reduction opportunities and more growth potential for the portfolio, the analysts say. Schaeffler's strategies should support resilient performance this year. But governance, leverage and liquidity weigh on the story. Its valuation is also less compelling than peers, they say. Shares trade 0.3% higher at 4.55 euros. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0900 GMT - Forvia's shares have again underperformed this year, but upcoming asset sales could finally help reduce large debt, Jefferies analysts say. The asset sales also represent an opportunity for the French auto-parts supplier to refresh the strategy to focus on what is essential, the analysts say. "We estimate 65% of fiscal 2024 sales will drive more than 120% of organic growth over the next three years," they say in a research note. Electronics and Seating sit at the heart of what is driving growth in the industry, and a return to outperformance in China in the second half of fiscal 2025 should be supportive for sentiment, they add. Shares trade 6% higher at 9.27 euros. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0848 GMT - Xiaomi's EV production capacity is the key bottleneck amid strong orders for its latest SUV, HSBC Global Research analysts write in a note. It would take more than three months for Xiaomi to ramp up its Yu7 production, leveraging both its phase 1 and phase 2 manufacturing plants, they say. The brokerage forecasts around 50,000 YU7 deliveries by September and a normalized run-rate of more than 20,000 vehicles a month in 4Q, they add. The YU7's prices were below market expectations, ranging from CNY253,500 to CNY329,900. The stronger-than-expected demand for the new model is likely due to competitive pricing, specs and a variety of customization solutions, they say. HSBC Global Research maintains its buy rating for the stock and raises the target price to HK$80.40 from HK$76.60. Shares last closed at HK$60.15. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

Hang Seng Index Closes 0.6% Lower at 24069.94
Synopsys Reports Lifting of US Export Restrictions Related to China; Shares Jump Premarket
data.description:
Synopsys said the Bureau of Industry and Security of the US Department of Commerce informed the company that export restrictions related to China have been "rescinded," effective immediately.

The company is working to restore access to the recently restricted products in China, it said late Wednesday.

"Synopsys is continuing to assess the impact of export restrictions related to China on its business, operating results, and financials," it added.

Shares of the company jumped more than 5% in premarket activity Thursday.

data.title: Datadog to Join S&P 500 July 9; Shares up Pre-Bell
data.description:
Datadog will join the S&P 500 July 9, S&P Global (SPGI) division S&P Dow Jones Indices said late Wednesday.

Datadog will replace Juniper Networks, which was recently acquired by Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE).

Datadog shares were up 12% in recent premarket activity Thursday.
